title: Why Physical Illness Reactivates Trauma Responses
researcherTopic: the body keeps the score, trauma and the brain
metaDescription: Physical illness can reactivate past trauma. Learn why this happens and how to navigate the experience of a dysregulated nervous system during illness.
imageDescription: A delicate, luminous web of golden threads, representing the nervous system, glows warmly against a soft, out-of-focus background. The light emanates from the center, suggesting healing and integration. The overall feeling is one of peace and resilience.
finalH2: The Invitation to a Deeper Integration
htmlContent:
Bessel van der Kolk’s work reminds us that the body keeps a score, a faithful and often brutal accounting of life events that the conscious mind might prefer to forget. This is a truth many discover not in a therapist’s office, but in a doctor’s, when a physical illness mysteriously resurrects the ghosts of a past trauma. The answer lies in the complex, non-verbal language of the body, where the past is never truly past, and where the sensations of physical vulnerability are indistinguishable from the existential threats of long ago.

The Body's Unspoken Memory

The architecture of a trauma response is not built from narrative but from sensation, from the raw, unprocessed data of an experience that overwhelmed the system’s capacity to cope. Unlike ordinary memories, which are encoded, contextualized, and filed away by the hippocampus, traumatic memories are often fragmented, stored implicitly in the body’s tissues, muscles, and autonomic nervous system. They exist as a kind of somatic shorthand - a tightness in the chest, a knot in the stomach, a sudden flush of heat - that bypasses the cognitive brain entirely. What we find here is because the body’s memory is not concerned with stories; it is concerned with survival. It remembers the feeling of being helpless, of being trapped, of being in pain, and it does not always differentiate between the then and the now.

When a physical illness arises, it speaks to the nervous system in this same primal, sensory language. The fatigue, the pain, the loss of control, the sheer vulnerability of being unwell ~ all of these sensations can be interpreted by the amygdala, the brain’s smoke detector, as a recurrence of the original threat. The body doesn’t hear the doctor’s calm explanation; it feels the echo of the past emergency. It remembers what it felt like to have its boundaries breached, its autonomy compromised, its very existence threatened. The result is a cascade of survival responses... We might find ourselves inexplicably enraged at a nurse, terrified of a routine procedure, or sinking into a significant, dissociative numbness. This isn’t a psychological failure; it is the body’s memory system running its programming with perfect fidelity. It is proof of the deep truth that we are not haunted by memories, but by the lingering, uncompleted physiological responses of the past. For more on how the body stores these experiences, one might explore the topic of somatic experiencing and its approach to healing.

The Erosion of the Rebuilt World

For many who have navigated the long road of recovery from complex trauma, life becomes a carefully constructed edifice built on a foundation of routines, predictions, and a hard-won sense of control. We build careers, relationships, and internal systems designed to keep the chaos at bay, creating a world that feels, if not entirely safe, then at least manageable. A physical illness, however, works like a seismic event, sending tremors through this painstakingly assembled structure and threatening to bring it all down. Suddenly, the future is no longer a territory one can map, but a vast, uncertain terrain shrouded in fog. The body, once a reliable vehicle, becomes a source of betrayal and unpredictability, its strange new symptoms and limitations a constant, visceral reminder of one's own fragility.

This collapse of perceived safety is so potent because it mirrors the original environment of the trauma itself ~ the environment where caregivers were unpredictable, where danger could erupt without warning, where the world was at its core not a trustworthy place. A client once described this experience to me as feeling like she had spent years learning to walk on a tightrope, only to have someone set the rope on fire from both ends. The skills, the balance, the focus - none of it seemed to matter when the very ground beneath her was disintegrating. It reactivates the core wound of C-PTSD: the belief that one is at the root unsafe and powerless in the face of overwhelming forces. Understanding this connection is crucial, as it is often the first step in learning about reparenting the parts of you that feel so young and terrified in the face of illness.

Let's talk about sensory triggers specifically. The smell of a certain cologne. The sound of a particular song. The texture of a fabric. The quality of light at a certain time of day. These aren't "just" memories. They're stored in the most primitive parts of your brain - the amygdala, the olfactory bulb, the thalamus - and they bypass all rational processing.

Bessel van der Kolk's brain imaging studies showed this clearly. When trauma survivors are exposed to triggers, the Broca's area - the part of the brain responsible for speech - goes offline. Literally. The language center shuts down. That's why you can't explain what's happening when you're triggered. That's why "use your words" is such an impossible demand in that state. There are no words available. Only sensation.

If this resonates with you, I want you to know something. You're not crazy. You're not weak. You're not making it up. Your brain is doing exactly what it was designed to do - protect you from threats it has encountered before. The work isn't to stop the triggering. The work is to help your nervous system update its files. To learn that the smell, the sound, the texture - they belong to then. And then is over. Even if your body hasn't gotten the memo yet.

A compassionate reframing of trauma that resonates with many is What Happened to You? (paid link).

The Nervous System's Echo Chamber

Sit with that.

This physiological amplification loop creates a significant sense of alienation from one's own body. It begins to feel like a treacherous territory, a place of constant, unpredictable danger. We start to mistrust its signals, treating every ache and pain as a harbinger of doom. This hypervigilant scanning of internal states is not a choice; it is a deeply ingrained survival strategy. The body is desperately trying to protect itself by anticipating the worst, a mechanism that was once essential for navigating a dangerous environment. The very attempt to find safety through constant monitoring creates a state of perpetual, low-grade alarm. It is a painful paradox, and one that many medical professionals, focused solely on the physical diagnosis, completely miss. They see a patient who seems overly anxious or emotional, failing to recognize that they are witnessing a nervous system caught in a powerful echo of the past. For those struggling with this, exploring resources on trauma-informed care can be validating, and organizations like the International Society for the Study of Trauma and Dissociation offer deeper insights into these complex presentations.

The Dissolution of the Witness

In the midst of a physical health crisis, the capacity for self-awareness - the ability to stand as a calm witness to one’s own experience... can feel like an impossible luxury. The sheer intensity of physical symptoms and the accompanying emotional dysregulation can collapse the space between the observer and the observed. We are the pain, we are the fear, we are the exhaustion. This fusion with the raw data of suffering is a hallmark of a trauma response, a regression to a state where there is no observing self, only a reactor. It is a form of functional dissociation, a survival mechanism designed to protect the organism from an unbearable reality by shutting down the part of the mind that is capable of reflecting on it.

A book that many trauma survivors find genuinely helpful is The Body Keeps the Score (paid link).

The road back from this state of fusion is not a heroic leap but a series of small, incremental movements toward re-establishing an internal locus of control. It is the practice of noticing, however fleetingly, that there is a part of you that is aware of the pain, a part that is aware of the fear. That awareness, in itself, is the seed of separation, the beginning of the return to a state where you are more than the sum of your symptoms. It requires a radical redefinition of what it means to be “in control,” moving away from the management of symptoms and toward the cultivation of a steady, compassionate presence that can endure the storm. It is a process that is often supported by frameworks like Internal Family Systems, which can be explored through resources like the IFS Institute.

The Invitation to a Deeper Integration

While the reactivation of trauma through physical illness is plainly brutal, it also carries within it a significant, if unwelcome, invitation. It is an invitation to a deeper level of integration, one that moves beyond the cognitive and into the very fabric of our physiology. It forces us to confront the ways in which we have been living at a distance from our bodies, treating them as machines to be managed rather than as integral aspects of our being. these are not just symptoms to be eradicated; they are messengers from the deepest parts of ourselves, parts that have been waiting, patiently, for an opportunity to be heard, to be met, to be finally brought home. This perspective does not romanticize the suffering, but it does refuse to pathologize it. It recognizes that the body, in its infinite wisdom, is always moving toward wholeness, even when its methods feel destructive.

Ultimately, the experience of illness becomes a crucible for a more embodied spirituality, one that is forged not in peak experiences but in the humble, often messy, reality of being human in a fragile body. It teaches us that a felt sense of safety is not a permanent state to be achieved, but a moment-to-moment practice of returning, of anchoring, of choosing to meet what is here with a presence that is both fierce and tender. It is the long, slow, beautiful work of mending the artificial split between mind and body, and in doing so, coming to a wholeness that is more resilient, more authentic, and more significantly alive than anything we could have constructed in the absence of the wound.

Frequently Asked Questions

Why does my anxiety get so much worse when I'm sick?

When you're physically ill, your body is in a state of physiological stress, which can activate your sympathetic nervous system ~ the same system responsible for the fight-or-flight response. For a trauma survivor, whose nervous system is already sensitized to threat, the sensations of illness (like a racing heart, fever, or fatigue) can be misinterpreted as signs of imminent danger, triggering a cascade of anxiety and panic that feels disproportionate to the illness itself. It's the body's memory of past helplessness being mapped onto the present vulnerability of being sick.

A guided journal specifically designed for trauma recovery is Trauma Recovery Journal (paid link).

Think about that for a second.

Is it possible to heal from this reactivation?

Yes, though it requires a shift in focus from simply getting over the illness to tending to the underlying trauma response that has been unearthed. This often involves somatic (body-based) therapies that help the nervous system complete old survival responses and learn to differentiate between past threat and present safety. The goal is not to prevent activation, but to increase your capacity to work through it with more ease, building a more resilient and regulated internal foundation over time.

The path is not one of erasure, but of integration. It is the slow and steady work of teaching the body, on its own terms, that the war is over. It is the practice of meeting the echoes of the past with the truth of the present, a truth that whispers, again and again, that you are here, you are now, and you are, finally, safe enough to heal.

A grounding tool that many find helpful during anxious moments is Spiky Sensory Ring Set (paid link).

You have been avoiding this. You know what I mean.
